skill.name: kaizen
skill.description: Guides incremental code improvement, error proofing, standardization, and just-in-time engineering decisions.
skill.summary: Apply Kaizen principles to improve code quality through small, verified changes.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, environment, weak-crypto, and reconnaissance patterns, but review found them inside Markdown guidance and TypeScript examples in SKILL.md. No executable script files, prompt injection attempts, credential exfiltration logic, or malicious intent were found, so the alerts are treated as false positives with low residual risk.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Misclassified as External Commands
low_findings.description: The external command alerts correspond to TypeScript code fences, arithmetic examples, and named slash commands in documentation. SKILL.md is the only file in the package, and no executable shell, Ruby, or script file is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged locations are inside Markdown examples or command references, not runnable files. No evidence found of actual shell execution or command injection behavior.

low_findings.title: Instructional Network Examples Only
low_findings.description: The fetch-related alerts are examples showing existing API client patterns and retry documentation. They do not execute during skill loading and do not send credentials or user data to an external endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network calls appear only in fenced TypeScript and documentation comments. No evidence found of live network code or data exfiltration intent.

low_findings.title: Instructional Environment Variable Example Only
low_findings.description: The API_KEY reference is a configuration validation example that demonstrates failing early when required configuration is absent. It is not executable package code and does not transmit the secret anywhere.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The process.env.API_KEY use is embedded in a marked example about configuration error proofing. No evidence found that the skill reads environment variables at runtime.

low_findings.title: Weak-Crypto and Reconnaissance Alerts Lack Supporting Context
low_findings.description: The scanner matched words related to design-time error prevention, codebase search, and named analysis commands. Review found no cryptographic implementation, credential harvesting, system profiling, or host reconnaissance logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious terms are part of ordinary software quality guidance and command labels. No evidence found of weak cryptographic algorithms or reconnaissance actions.

content.value_statement: Teams often make risky, oversized changes when improving code. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code toward small verified improvements, safer types, shared standards, and practical scope control.
actual_capabilities: Guides incremental implementation and refactoring with verification after each change.
Promotes type-safe designs that make invalid states harder to represent.
Recommends early validation, guard clauses, and clear failure modes.
Encourages consistent project patterns, documentation, and quality gates.
Helps avoid premature abstraction, optimization, and speculative features.
Points users toward structured improvement commands such as root cause and cycle analysis.
limitations: Provides guidance only; it does not run automated refactors or tests by itself.
Does not include executable tooling, scripts, or project-specific analyzers.
Requires the user or agent to inspect the actual codebase before applying recommendations.
May be too general for teams that need a strict regulatory or formal methods process.

use_cases.title: Refactor a Risky Module Gradually
use_cases.description: Break a large cleanup into small, testable steps that keep behavior stable.

use_cases.title: Improve Team Coding Standards
use_cases.description: Align new work with existing patterns, documentation, and quality gates.

use_cases.title: Design Safer APIs
use_cases.description: Use types, validation, and preconditions to prevent common runtime mistakes.

prompt_templates.title: Find One Small Improvement
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a limited improvement pass.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Kaizen skill to review this file. Suggest one small improvement that reduces risk and explain how to verify it.

prompt_templates.title: Refactor in Verified Steps
prompt_templates.prompt: Apply Kaizen principles to refactor this module in small steps. Keep each step testable and stop before speculative changes.

prompt_templates.title: Error-Proof an Interface
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request for safer API or data model design.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Poka-Yoke guidance to redesign this interface so invalid states are harder to create. Include validation boundaries and tradeoffs.

prompt_templates.title: Create an Improvement Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Kaizen and standardized work to create a practical improvement plan for this workflow. Include priorities, verification checks, and stop conditions.

output_examples.input: A user asks for a broad refactor of a payment module.
output: Run focused verification before the next change.
Defer unrelated abstractions until repeated needs appear.

output_examples.input: A team wants to reduce production configuration errors.
output: Move validation to startup or request boundaries.
Use clear errors for missing or invalid values.
Document the standard so future services follow it.

output_examples.input: A developer wants to optimize a slow path without measurements.
output_examples.output: The skill would recommend profiling first, recording the baseline, and changing only the measured bottleneck.

best_practices: Start with the smallest change that improves quality and can be verified quickly.
Use the existing codebase conventions before introducing a new pattern.
Validate inputs at boundaries and make invalid states difficult to express.
anti_patterns: Do not combine refactoring, feature work, optimization, and new abstractions in one change.
Do not add frameworks or generic layers for needs that are only hypothetical.
Do not rely on comments or user caution when types or validation can prevent errors.

faq.question: Does this skill modify code automatically?
faq.answer: No. It guides the AI toward safer incremental changes, but actual edits depend on the user request and available tools.

faq.question: Is this only for refactoring?
faq.answer: No. It also supports architecture, validation, error handling, documentation standards, and workflow improvement.

faq.question: Can it help with test strategy?
faq.answer: Yes. It encourages verification after each small change and keeping tests passing throughout refactoring.

faq.question: Does it replace security review?
faq.answer: No. It improves design discipline but does not perform a complete security audit by itself.

faq.question: Why does the audit show low risk?
faq.answer: The suspicious patterns are documentation examples in SKILL.md, not executable code or live network behavior.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
